title: How to Work with Nausea During the Come-Up Phase
body:
How to Work with Nausea During the Come-Up Phase

So you’re sitting there, maybe on your couch or in your favorite spot in nature, and suddenly the nausea hits. That queasy, twisting feeling in your stomach just as your psychedelic journey begins to unfold. You’re not alone. A lot of people hit this wall during the come-up phase - whether it’s from psilocybin, LSD, or other substances. It’s frustrating. It can throw you off before the real exploration even starts. But here’s the thing: nausea doesn’t have to ruin the ride. You can work with it. You can even ease it.

Let’s unpack this. I want to give you real, practical steps that you can try the next time you feel that stomach churn. No fluff, no spiritual bypassing. Just grounded advice, drawn from experience and science. Ready? Let’s get into it.

Why Does Nausea Even Happen?

First off, nausea during the come-up isn’t random. There’s a reason your gut’s flipping out. Psychedelics affect serotonin receptors, which are abundant not only in your brain but also in your digestive system. When they activate, your gut can get a little too excited, triggering nausea or even vomiting. Plus, your body’s stress response might kick in - sometimes you’re simply anxious or tense about what’s about to happen. Know what I mean?

Plus, if you took your substance on a full stomach or combined it with other things like alcohol or heavy food, your digestive system might rebel. It’s a perfect storm. But nausea during the come-up is common enough that researchers have been paying close attention. If you want to nerd out (and I get that), check out this study on serotonin receptor influence on gut motility. It’s fascinating.

Step One: Breathe Into It

This might sound basic, but trust me, it’s worth saying. When nausea strikes, your first reaction might be to tense up, hold your breath, or freak out a little. Instead, pause. Take a deep breath. In through your nose for four seconds, hold for four, out through your mouth for six. Repeat this five times. Breathing deeply activates your parasympathetic nervous system - the part of your brain that calms you down. It can reduce nausea significantly.

Don’t just take my word for it. A friend of mine, Lisa, swears by this whenever she microdoses psilocybin and feels queasy. “It’s like a reset button,” she told me. “I focus on my breath, and the nausea fades enough to enjoy the experience. It’s not magic, but it works.” Does that land?

Step Two: Mind Your Position

Body positioning can make or break your nausea. If you’re sitting upright, your stomach might feel worse. Lie down on your left side if you can. This position helps your stomach empty more easily and reduces the feeling of queasiness. If you’re outside, find a comfortable spot to recline, maybe with your head slightly elevated. Avoid slouching or curling tightly - it can make nausea worse.

One of my students, Tom, shared a moment where he felt trapped by nausea after an intense microdose. He shifted to his left side, closed his eyes, and the nausea became manageable. Sometimes small shifts make a big difference.

Step Three: Hydrate Smartly

Hydration is tricky. You don’t want to gulp a ton of water fast because that can worsen nausea. Instead, sip slowly and steadily. Warm herbal teas like ginger or peppermint can soothe your stomach. Ginger, specifically, has been shown in multiple studies to reduce nausea in different contexts, including chemotherapy and motion sickness. It’s a natural remedy that’s easy to try.

If you want to keep it simple, plain warm water works too. Avoid sugary drinks or caffeine during this phase - they often make your stomach feel worse. Keep a bottle nearby and take small sips every few minutes.

Step Four: Distract and Ground Your Mind

Honestly, nausea is partly a mind game. When you fixate on it, it grows. When you gently shift your focus, it often diminishes. Try grounding techniques to anchor yourself. Touch a textured object - a soft blanket, a smooth stone, or even your own skin. Naming five things you see, four things you hear, three things you feel can pull you out of the spiral.

One method I recommend is journaling your sensations. Grab a guided meditation journal (paid link) and jot down what you’re feeling. Sometimes putting nausea into words takes away some of its power.

Step Five: Use Gentle Movement

While intense movement might be the last thing you want, gentle, slow motion can help. Try a short, mindful walk if you feel steady enough. Movement stimulates your digestive tract and can ease nausea. Even rocking gently back and forth while sitting or swaying side to side might help reset your system.

But here’s the thing: listen to your body. If movement increases your nausea, stop immediately. There’s no one-size-fits-all approach here.

Step Six: Consider Supplements and Tools

If nausea is a recurring issue for you, there are some tools and supplements that might help. Ginger capsules or ginger chews can be handy if you’re out and about. Peppermint essential oil inhalation offers quick relief as well.

On the tech side, tracking your physiological state can offer insight. I’ve been using a WHOOP HRV monitor (paid link) to tune into my nervous system during sessions. By noticing heart rate variability changes, I can anticipate when nausea might hit and take preventive steps.

Step Seven: Prep Your Environment

Light, sound, and temperature can all affect nausea. Bright lights or screens make my stomach flare up. That’s why I wear blue light blocking glasses (paid link) when I’m coming up. It reduces sensory strain.

Keep your space cool but not cold. Stuffy heat can worsen nausea. Have fresh air circulating, whether that’s a window cracked open or a fan running softly. If noise is an issue, use soft ambient sounds or white noise to mask anything jarring.

Step Eight: Don’t Fight the Nausea

This one’s tough. When nausea hits hard, your instinct is to resist. But sometimes fighting it makes it worse. Instead, acknowledge it. “Okay, nausea, I see you.” Sit with it, breathe into it. Let it flow rather than stiffening up against it. This isn’t about spiritual bypassing - it’s about acceptance.

That said, if nausea escalates to vomiting and you’re feeling unsafe, call for help or have a trusted sitter close by. Always prioritize your safety.

When to Seek Help

If nausea comes with sharp abdominal pain, severe dizziness, or confusion, that’s a red flag. These symptoms don’t belong to a typical psychedelic come-up and require medical attention. This is non-negotiable.

Otherwise, nausea that peaks and fades within the first hour or so is usually part of the process. Having a trusted friend or sitter aware of what you’re experiencing can provide reassurance and practical help.

Long Term: Building Tolerance and Understanding

Some people find nausea lessens with experience. That’s probably because your body and nervous system get used to the effects and you learn how to manage the sensations better. If nausea consistently ruins your experience, consider adjusting your dose, timing, or method of ingestion.

For example, microdosing psilocybin seems to trigger less nausea than a full trip dose, but everyone’s body is different. Also, note that your mindset and environment before you take a psychedelic can influence nausea. Stress, hunger, dehydration, or pre-existing gut issues all play a role.

If nausea persists or feels tied to deeper trauma or unresolved physical issues, I recommend reading The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It’s a powerful book about how our bodies hold pain and how healing can happen when we listen carefully.

Final Thoughts

Look, nausea during the come-up is a real and valid challenge. It can be disorienting and uncomfortable. But you don’t have to just suffer through it. By breathing, adjusting your body, hydrating carefully, grounding your mind, moving gently, prepping your environment, and sometimes even using tools or supplements, you can work with nausea rather than against it.

And remember: the come-up is just one phase. After it passes, the journey often opens up in new, unexpected ways. If you want more tips on managing different parts of your psychedelic experience, check out my article on Handling Challenging Trips. Does that land?

If you want to explore more about conscious healing and psychedelic wellness, swing by kalesh.love. I’m always sharing insights, resources, and stories from the frontlines.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
